old_claim.note: Early Boats is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Manual propulsion is a boat-operating method and requires a watercraft platform; the Egyptian evidence describes boats with oars before and alongside sails.

source_shape.source_locator: Oars and sails section: describes Egyptian boat evidence from the fourth millennium BCE and early Nile boats using oars and sails.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes manually propelled Egyptian boats, tying oars to the operation of boats before and alongside sails.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the scoped technology is manual propulsion of watercraft, a boat platform is not merely an earlier historical context; it is the object being propelled.

ontology_before: The graph treated Early Boats as a loose historical predecessor for Basic Boat Propulsion.
ontology_after: Manual Boat Propulsion is scoped as an operating method on boats, so early_boats is the required platform context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: early_boats remains connected but is retyped from historical_predecessor to required.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to standalone paddle manufacture rather than operating boats.
A source showed the scoped manual-propulsion practice did not require a watercraft platform.
The node were split so that oars, paddles, poles, and steering oars became separate technologies with different prerequisites.
short_reason: Manual propulsion is a boat-operating method, so a boat platform is required.
